attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic. She is 36 weeks pregnant with her first child. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Antenatal examination reveals a cephalic fetus, 3/5 head palpable, and fetal heart rate 145 beats/min. She describes feeling tired and lethargic, but pregnancy has been progressing well. Her midwife suggested starting oral iron two months ago, which she has found hard to tolerate due to constipation. Fetal movements have been normal. She denies any headaches or visual symptoms. Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin: 105 g/L (110-165)
Mean cell volume: 75 fL (80-100)
Ferritin: 6 µg/L (15-250)
Transferrin saturation: 8% (10-35)
Vitamin B12: 280 pmol/L (150-650)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy. The iron studies show depleted iron stores, with a ferritin of 6 µg/L (well below the 30 µg/L threshold for iron deficiency in pregnancy) and low transferrin saturation. Her haemoglobin is 105 g/L, indicating anaemia, and her mean cell volume is low, indicating microcytosis.

Intravenous iron infusion is the most appropriate management in this case. Given the developing anaemia (Hb 105 g/L), the late stage of pregnancy (36 weeks), and the patient's difficulty tolerating oral iron, intravenous iron is indicated. It allows for rapid repletion of iron stores and correction of anaemia, which is crucial as the patient approaches delivery.

Ongoing oral iron supplementation would be insufficient in this case. While it is the first-line treatment for mild iron deficiency in pregnancy, this patient has moderate anaemia and is approaching term. Oral iron would not correct the anaemia quickly enough before delivery.

Change oral iron formulation might help with tolerability issues, but would still not address the need for rapid correction of anaemia at this late stage of pregnancy.

Intramuscular vitamin B12 injection is not indicated in this case. The patient's vitamin B12 level is normal, and her symptoms and blood results are consistent with iron deficiency rather than B12 deficiency.

Intramuscular iron injection is rarely used in modern obstetric practice due to unpredictable absorption, risk of local complications, and the availability of safer intravenous alternatives.

The key to management is recognising the severity of iron deficiency anaemia based on the low haemoglobin and ferritin, especially at this late stage of pregnancy. Intravenous iron infusion allows for rapid correction of anaemia and repletion of iron stores, which is crucial as the patient approaches delivery. It also bypasses the gastrointestinal side effects that the patient experienced with oral iron.
learningPoints: Iron deficiency anaemia is common in pregnancy and should be screened for at the first antenatal visit and at 28 weeks gestation.
Ferritin is the most sensitive and specific test for iron deficiency in pregnancy.
Intravenous iron infusion is indicated for severe iron deficiency anaemia, especially when oral iron is not tolerated or ineffective, or when rapid repletion is needed late in pregnancy.
references: Ganesan K, McCarthy EA, Barlow P, Walker SP. Anaemia in pregnancy: Evaluation and management. Aust J Gen Pract. 2019;48(3):125-9. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/march/anaemia-in-pregnancy
Cutts B. Q&A: Iron infusions in pregnancy. O&G Magazine [Internet]. 2016 [cited 2024 Oct 19];18(2). Available from: https://www.ogmagazine.org.au/18/2-18/qa-2/
